title: Policy conflicts & the Phillips curve
summary:
## Objectives that pull against each other

Governments rarely achieve all macro objectives at once — pursuing one often worsens another. This chapter analyses the main **conflicts** and the **Phillips curve**, the classic model of the inflation–unemployment trade-off.

## The main policy conflicts

- **Unemployment vs inflation** — cutting unemployment by boosting AD can raise **demand-pull inflation** (the Phillips curve).
- **Economic growth vs inflation** — fast demand-led growth near capacity is inflationary.
- **Economic growth vs the current account** — growth raises incomes and **sucks in imports**, worsening the trade balance.
- **Economic growth vs the environment / sustainability** — output growth can raise pollution and deplete resources.
- **Growth/efficiency vs equity** — supply-side reforms and growth can widen **inequality**.
- **Short run vs long run** — deflating to cut inflation raises unemployment now; supply-side reform helps later but not immediately.

## The Phillips curve

### Short-run Phillips curve (SRPC)
A.W. Phillips found an **inverse relationship** between unemployment and inflation: lower unemployment ↔ higher inflation. This implies a **menu of choices** — governments could ‘trade’ some inflation for lower unemployment via demand management.

### The breakdown — stagflation
The 1970s saw **stagflation** (high inflation *and* high unemployment), which the simple curve couldn't explain. Friedman and Phelps responded with the expectations-augmented curve.

### Long-run Phillips curve (LRPC)
- Workers adjust to **expected inflation**, so any attempt to hold unemployment below the **natural rate (NRU/NAIRU)** only causes **accelerating inflation**.
- The **LRPC is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment** — the monetarist mirror of the vertical LRAS.
- So there is **no long-run trade-off**: unemployment below the NRU is unsustainable. Only **supply-side policies that lower the NRU** can cut unemployment without inflation.

## Managing conflicts

- **Combine policies** — e.g. loose monetary policy plus supply-side reform.
- **Prioritise** according to the current problem and the government's aims.
- **Independent central banks / inflation targeting** anchor expectations and reduce the trade-off's severity.
- **Supply-side policy** is the key to easing conflicts because it can improve *several* objectives at once (growth, jobs, inflation, competitiveness).

## Why it matters

Policy conflicts are the essence of top-band macro evaluation. The Phillips curve integrates AD/AS, expectations and the natural rate, and shows why supply-side improvement is central to escaping the trade-offs.

keyTerms.definition: When achieving one macro objective worsens another.
The inverse short-run relationship between unemployment and inflation.
A vertical line at the natural rate of unemployment, implying no long-run trade-off.
The unemployment rate consistent with stable inflation, determined by supply-side factors.
The simultaneous occurrence of high inflation and high unemployment.
A Phillips curve that shifts with workers' inflation expectations.

diagrams: **Short-run Phillips curve:** downward-sloping, inflation on the y-axis and unemployment on the x-axis.

**Long-run Phillips curve:** a vertical LRPC at the natural rate, with a family of SRPCs shifting up as expected inflation rises; show the move from A to B to C when unemployment is pushed below the NRU.
Pair with **AD/AS** to link the Phillips curve to demand-pull inflation.
tips: Have the **main conflicts** ready as an evaluation checklist (unemployment–inflation, growth–current account, growth–environment, efficiency–equity).
Explain the **SRPC vs LRPC** and why the long-run curve is **vertical at the NRU** (expectations adjust).
Use **stagflation** to explain why the original Phillips curve broke down.
Conclude that **only supply-side policy that lowers the NRU** cuts unemployment without inflation.
Mention **inflation targeting / independent central banks** as ways to soften the trade-off.
